frameworks.name: temporal discounting
frameworks.one_line: subjective value of a reward decays with delay to its delivery

frameworks.name: expected utility / risk preference
frameworks.one_line: utility curvature over outcomes treated as separable construct shaping discounting tasks

frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: frames choice impulsivity as a quantitative trans-diagnostic phenotype

core_question: How do risk preferences and time preferences interact mathematically and neurally to shape measurements of choice impulsivity in psychiatric populations?
key_assumptions: choice impulsivity is a subcomponent of broader impulsivity
risk and time preferences are behaviourally and neurobiologically distinct constructs
subjective value can be decomposed into risk-utility and delay-discounting components
key_findings: risk attitude can confound nearly every standard temporal-discounting assay
risk preference and time preference dissociate behaviourally and neurally despite shared subjective-value computation
interpretations: psychiatric research on impulsivity should jointly model risk and time preferences to recover clean discounting estimates
accounting for this interaction improves the clinical interpretation of choice impulsivity
limitations_noted: opinion piece rather than primary empirical study
